pattern: Once a person's access times are converted to minutes and sorted, a 60-minute window containing three uses is just a check on every group of three consecutive sorted timestamps, since any wider gap could only make a triple's span larger, never smaller.
hints: Convert every 'HH:MM' timestamp into a single integer number of minutes since midnight, then group all of a single person's timestamps together.
Sort each person's timestamps; if any three uses within a 60-minute window exist, the tightest such window is always three timestamps that are consecutive once sorted.
For each person, scan their sorted timestamps in triples (index i and i+2); if the gap between them is at most 60, that person gets an alert. Collect and sort the alerted names alphabetically.

edge_inputs.description: one person alerts, one does not, from the classic three-uses-in-an-hour case
unsorted input times must still be handled correctly
exactly two uses, never enough to alert
exactly 60 minutes apart counts as within the window, not outside it
just over 60 minutes apart must not alert
multiple people, only names that actually qualify are returned, sorted

stress.description: many people with random access times over a single day
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    names = [''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(rng.randint(1, 6))) for _ in range(max(1, n // 10))]
    key_name = []
    key_time = []
    for _ in range(n):
        name = rng.choice(names)
        h = rng.randint(0, 23)
        m = rng.randint(0, 59)
        key_name.append(name)
        key_time.append(f'{h:02d}:{m:02d}')
    return [key_name, key_time]
